text:
Mode: **stress**, depth: **standard** — both specified, so we can skip setup and go straight to the thesis.

Here's my restatement of your idea, as strongly as I can put it:

> "Support tickets currently require a human to read and route them; an LLM can read incoming tickets and automatically classify/prioritize/assign them, cutting the manual triage step and getting tickets to the right owner faster."

Is that an accurate, strongest-form statement of what you want to build — or is there a piece I'm over- or under-stating (e.g. does "triage" mean just categorization, or does it also include drafting a response, closing duplicates, escalating urgent ones)?

Yeah, that's basically it — but I'd push it further than just categorize-and-assign. The whole point is the bot also auto-closes the easy, low-value tickets on its own, not just routes them to a human. If it's only doing triage and a person still has to act on every ticket, we haven't actually saved anyone time.

Updated thesis, incorporating that correction:

> "An LLM-powered bot should not just categorize and route support tickets to the right human — it should autonomously resolve and close the easy, low-value tickets itself, with humans only handling what's left. The time savings come specifically from removing human action on a subset of tickets, not just speeding up routing."

Is that the corrected thesis — accurate and complete now?
Yeah, that's accurate.

Thesis confirmed. Starting the questioning — one question at a time, stress mode.

**Clarification:** What actually makes a ticket "easy, low-value" enough for the bot to close it without a human ever looking at it? Can you point to a real example from your current queue — one specific ticket you'd have wanted the bot to auto-close last week?

We haven't actually been logging real examples yet, but a good candidate would be something like a "how do I reset my password" ticket, or a "what's my order status" lookup — stuff where the answer is a known FAQ and there's no account risk or judgment call involved.

**Counterexample:** Password reset is actually one of the classic vectors for account-takeover — attackers impersonate legitimate users in support channels specifically to get a password reset pushed through. If the bot is empowered to resolve that ticket type end-to-end with no human in the loop, how does it distinguish the real account owner from someone social-engineering it, given you just called that category "no account risk"?

We haven't nailed down the exact mechanism yet, but yeah, that's exactly the kind of risk that worries me — if the bot gets it wrong or a bad actor slips through, that's a real problem. Which is actually why every customer-facing reply, even the "easy" ones, still has to get signed off by a human before it goes out — compliance already burned us once on this, so that's a non-negotiable check regardless of how the bot classifies the ticket.

**Contradiction:** These two things you've said don't sit together. Earlier: *"If it's only doing triage and a person still has to act on every ticket, we haven't actually saved anyone time."* Just now: *"every customer-facing reply, even the 'easy' ones, still has to get signed off by a human before it goes out... that's a non-negotiable check regardless of how the bot classifies the ticket."*

If every reply needs human sign-off before it goes out, a human is acting on every ticket — which by your own earlier standard means the time isn't actually being saved. Which of these gives way: does the sign-off requirement bend for the low-value category, or does the "no time saved without full autonomy" claim not hold the way you first stated it?

Both stay. Compliance sign-off happens on the reply — the human isn't reading the ticket, triaging it, or deciding what to do with it, they're just confirming the drafted response before it goes out. That's a lighter check than full manual handling, and it can happen fast if the bot's draft is already right. The time savings come from the bot doing the reading, classifying, and drafting; the sign-off is a gate, not a redo of the work.

That reconciles it — a fast confirm-gate is a materially different claim than full manual handling, so the "no time saved" logic doesn't actually collide with the sign-off requirement. I'll fold "human reviews every reply, but only to confirm — not to triage or draft" into the thesis.

**Falsification:** What would tell you that gate is *not* staying lightweight — that it's quietly turning back into full manual work? Concretely: if reviewers end up editing or rejecting some percentage of the bot's drafts, is there a number where you'd say "this isn't actually saving time, it's just review theater"?

Yeah, that's the honest line to draw. If reviewers are editing or rejecting a big chunk of drafts — I'd say more than 20-30% — that tells me the bot isn't actually drafting well enough and we're just relabeling manual work as "review." At that point it's not a time-save, it's theater with extra steps.

Good — that's a real, checkable line, not just a vibe.

**Evidence & reasons:** You mentioned earlier you haven't been logging real examples yet. So what actually tells you the "known FAQ, no account risk, no judgment call" bucket is a meaningful chunk of your ticket volume — do you have any sense of what percentage of tickets would even qualify, or is that still a guess at this point?

We haven't run the numbers on that yet — it's a guess based on gut feel from skimming ticket subjects, not anything I've pulled from the queue. I'd want to actually tag a couple weeks of tickets before committing to a real percentage.

That's worth naming plainly: the volume assumption underneath this whole project — that there's a meaningfully large bucket of safe-to-auto-close tickets — hasn't been measured yet. It's currently gut feel from skimming subject lines.

**Assumptions:** You said the human sign-off is just confirming the drafted reply reads right, not re-triaging the ticket. So if the bot *misclassifies* a ticket as "easy, no account risk" when it actually wasn't — say, a disguised account-takeover attempt that reads like an FAQ question — does the human reviewer at the sign-off step have any way to catch that, or are they only ever judging "is this reply well-written," not "should this ticket have been auto-handled at all"?

No, they're not just judging whether it reads well — the reviewer sees the ticket and the draft reply side by side before confirming, so if it's clearly an account-recovery situation dressed up as an FAQ, that should be visible to them at sign-off, not just "does this sentence sound right."

**Contradiction:** Two turns ago you drew the line as: *"the human isn't reading the ticket, triaging it, or deciding what to do with it, they're just confirming the drafted response."* Just now, to catch a disguised risk case, you said: *"the reviewer sees the ticket and the draft reply side by side before confirming... that should be visible to them."*

But if the reviewer has to actually read the original ticket and judge whether it was the right call to auto-handle it — not just whether the sentence reads well — that sounds a lot like triage happening at sign-off, just with a draft already attached. Which is it: is the reviewer just confirming text, or are they still doing the judgment call on the ticket itself?

Both stay non-negotiable. The reviewer looking at the ticket to catch a misfire isn't the same as them doing triage — triage is deciding where a ticket should go and what should happen to it across the whole queue; sign-off is just one person looking at one ticket-and-draft pair and saying yes or no before it goes out. It's a judgment call, sure, but it's a bounded, fast one, not the open-ended "read and route everything" work the bot was supposed to take off our plate.

That distinction holds — a bounded "does this pairing look right, yes/no" judgment is a different kind of work than open-ended routing, even if a human still touches every ticket. I'll fold that precision into the brief.

**Questioning the question:** Stepping back — everything you've described puts a human eyes-on every single reply before it ships, just making a fast bounded call instead of drafting from scratch. Given that, is the real question here "should we build a bot that autonomously closes tickets," or is it actually "how do we get humans through ticket responses faster" — where an AI-drafting assistant with no autonomous send/close capability might get you most of the same time savings without ever needing to solve the account-risk and misclassification problems at all?

Look, that's a fair reframe, but I don't think it gets us out of the account-risk problem — a drafting assistant still needs the bot to correctly flag "this one's actually a takeover attempt" or the human reviewer's just as exposed. The autonomy piece matters because it's what actually removes work from the queue; if a human has to touch every single ticket to even see a draft, we're back to one-ticket-at-a-time throughput, just faster typing. I want the assistant version measured too, but I don't think it replaces the goal.

Fair — the autonomy piece is doing real work in your argument, and you've flagged the assistant-only version as worth measuring separately rather than as a replacement. I'll note that as a comparison worth running, not a competing thesis.

**Implications & consequences:** Say this works and the bot closes a ticket after sign-off. If the customer comes back a week later disputing that resolution — "that didn't actually fix my problem" — what happens? Is there a reopen path, and does responsibility for that sit with the human who signed off, the bot, or someone else?

There's a reopen path, yeah — the customer can flag it and it goes back into the queue, not just closed and forgotten. But responsibility sits with the human who signed off, not the bot — they're the one who confirmed the reply before it went out, so if it turns out that was the wrong call, that's on the review step, not "the AI made a mistake" as some kind of excuse. That's actually part of why the sign-off has to be a named individual, not a batch or a template — compliance wants someone accountable for each one, not a shared shrug.

Good — that's a meaningful constraint: sign-off has to be a named, accountable individual, not a batch approval, which is itself a design requirement the bot's UI/workflow has to support.

**Viewpoints & alternatives:** Who inside your org is most likely to push back on this, and what's the strongest version of their objection? I'm thinking specifically of the support agents themselves — would they describe this as taking the tedious stuff off their plate, or would they see it differently (loss of visibility into what's happening in their own queue, deskilling, "the bot gets the easy wins and I'm left with only the hard/frustrating tickets")?

Honestly? Support agents. They'd probably say the bot's cherry-picking the quick wins and leaving them with a queue that's all edge cases and angry customers, so their average ticket gets harder and their stats look worse even if total volume drops. And if sign-off duty gets spread onto them too, they might feel like they're babysitting the bot instead of actually being freed up.

That's a real cost that hasn't been priced in yet — total ticket volume could drop while the human experience of the job gets worse, and "sign-off duty" could read as busywork rather than relief.

One more: **Does that agent-experience risk change what you'd count as success here** — or is "total ticket volume handled without human drafting" still the headline metric even if it means the remaining human queue is harder and morale takes a hit?

No — total volume is still the headline number, but I wouldn't call it the *only* one. If agent morale tanks or the hard-ticket queue starts blowing SLAs, that's a real failure mode even if the raw count looks good. I'd want both tracked, but volume-without-drafting is still the thing that justifies building this in the first place.

The brief is written to `notes/idea-briefs/llm-support-ticket-triage-bot-20260721.md`. Self-check passed: all required frontmatter fields are present and enum-valid, `verdict: aporia` carries three non-empty `open_questions`, all four assumptions carry a status, and `questions_asked: 11` matches a recount of the dialogue.

**Summary:**

- **Verdict: aporia.** The thesis is sharp on scope and mechanics (autonomous read/classify/draft + mandatory named-individual sign-off gate), but three load-bearing gaps are still open, not just assumed:
  1. The volume of eligible "easy/low-risk" tickets is an unmeasured guess.
  2. The mechanism for catching a disguised risk ticket at sign-off isn't designed.
  3. There's no mitigation plan for the agent-morale/SLA risk, only agreement to track it.
- **What survived stress-testing:** the sign-off-vs-autonomy distinction, the account-risk counterexample (handled by mandatory human sign-off), the "assistant-only" reframe (rejected — autonomy is the point), and a concrete falsification line (>20–30% draft edit/reject rate = "review theater, not time savings").
- **Next step:** tag two weeks of real tickets to measure the actual eligible volume before committing further — that's the number the whole ROI case currently rests on.
